10 min read
In Part I of the Discover Process, you answered the question:
what is the skill or expertise I have, or can acquire, that a
POP/MVA will value enough to (likely) pay for?
The critical distinction here was to identify work (what really
interests you?) you can ‘fall in love’ doing long-term in service to a
pocket of people who are waiting for you to show up and help
create meaningful positive change for them.
Part I should have felt like a journey, where you discovered things
about yourself that may have never been obvious to you before.
For some of you, it may have felt like a therapy session.
With orientation from Part I, you now had a direction for Part II,
where the goal was to seek out your potential POP/MVA in the
places they hang out in the wild to observe their language and
behavior.
The Discover Process is not about solutions — it’s all about
discovering a problem worth solving (or desire you can help
Discover, Part III: Questions
Framework
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 14 --

ful�ll).
It’s easy to fall into the ‘solution trap’ (i.e., creating solutions �rst
and then looking for problems to solve). Identify the problems (or
desires) �rst (we’ll move on to solutions in the Validate Process).
In Part III, we’re going to amplify our ability to uncover problems
worth solving (that are not biased or colored with solution-
thinking) by moving from implicit observational research to
being more explicit in our interactions with our potential
POP/MVA.
Before we dive into Part III, we want to draw attention to the fact
that multiple paths lead to the same or similar outcome.
The challenge we’ve been wrestling with is the best set of ideas
and frameworks we can teach that will be broadly useful and
biased towards agility, speed, and minimizing complexity.
In essence, what does elegant simplicity look like when our goal is
to discover problems worth solving?
“I would not give a �g for the simplicity on this side of complexity; but I
would give my life for the simplicity on the far side of complexity.” —
U.S. Supreme Court Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 14 --

Image inspired by Carl Richards from Behavior Gap
The problem with complexity is that it can present a more
detailed and thorough work�ow, but the extra nuance and steps
required can be overwhelming and cause unwelcome friction that
stalls forward momentum.
In the end, we feel that less friction will serve us more effectively.
This means that Part III will likely play out a little differently for
everyone, but we’ll all get to the desired endpoint.
We’re going to use two student comments taken from Part I and
Part II to help frame this lesson and give real world texture to it.
In the comment below, Charlotte has already jumped ahead and
interviewed people who match the characteristics of her
POP/MVA.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 14 --

In the words of Steve Blank, the founder of the modern day lean
startup movement: “Rule No. 1: There are no facts inside your building,
so get outside.”
The concept of getting out of the building is simply to start
interacting and engaging with potential customers (an audience
that broadly represents the characteristics of the people you think
you may want to serve).
This doesn’t explicitly mean you need to get people on the phone,
or meet in-person, to ask them how they currently solve a
potential problem (or problems).
This may be the case, as with Charlotte, but, for the introverts out
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 14 --

there, it can also be asking ‘problem-focused’ questions on forums
and groups to elicit constructive dialogue and discussions around
the topic you seek to learn more about.
This is a good time to introduce the next student’s comment.
(To be clear, we’re not picking on Hugues. There are no bad questions. It’s
our job to help give clarity to the process, so we appreciate all questions,
especially ones like this.)
This is an excellent question from Hugues because it represents
an example of how easy it is to skip a step and bring a solution-
focused lens to the Discovery Process.
Adding the 35-50 year demographic layer presumes too much and
de�nes a customer segment too narrowly too quickly. Hugues has
a solution in mind already which is OK, but that’s something to be
Validated, not something to be Discovered.
This is the same issue with “don’t have time to join their local
classes” because it presumes a problem (that time is the reason for
not joining a local judo class) and is already implicitly framing a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 14 --

potential solution (online classes).
A better alternative would be for Hugues to begin with a clean
slate, and let the appropriate POP/MVA emerge based on what he
learns during the Discovery Process.
▪ What are people on Judo forums complaining about or
interested in (as it relates to their Judo practice)?
▪ What desires emerge consistently?
Capture that information, and then develop potential solutions to
those problems to Validate in the next step.
For example, let’s assume that a signi�cant number of Judokas
wish they could polish or sharpen their skills through remote
learning. That’s a great place to start. Once that thread has
emerged, follow those conversations.
▪ What are the desires associated with that need, as expressed
in the language of that audience?
▪ What objections are raised by others?
That’s where you’ll �nd veins of gold in the Discover Process.
Be curious: ask a question, then listen closely to the replies. Rise and
repeat.
This work is is not easy. We’re inclined to move quickly to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 14 --

solutions (especially when we have value we want to contribute to
the world). It can be challenging to override this tendency when
we go out and do our research. Be patient.
Let’s look at a two-part framework that’s broadly useful:
�. Phase 1: Seek to identify 1-3 signi�cant problems through
‘�y on the wall’ observational research (think jobs, pains, and
gains).
�. Phase 2: Now interact and engage with people who seem to
make up your ideal POP/MVA in places where they
congregate (for now, this will likely be exclusively online,
but in the future, this may include ‘of�ine’ in-person
interactions).
Phase 2 is about asking open-ended, non-leading questions to
tease out speci�c insights about your POP/MVA.
For Hugues, instead of starting from a constraint of “where can I
�nd and reach men between ages 35-50?” he would be better
served by inserting himself into a broad range of judo groups and
asking a variation of this theme, as one of them:
Hey guys, I would love some advice on a problem I’ve been wrestling
with over the past few months. COVID has had a wide-ranging impact
on group activities like martial arts and judo.
Although some judo clubs have started accepting some higher-ranking
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 14 --

members back in small groups, I’ve found it challenging for someone
who is just a recreational enthusiast who practices judo for �tness and
wellbeing.
1.) Am I alone thinking this, or are there others feeling this way too?
2.) And if so, how are you dealing with this?
Best, Hugues
�	�	�	�	�	�	�	�	�
This is not meant to be a template but rather highlight the idea of
asking questions that can open up avenues for dialogue. We want
people jumping in, contributing to the discussion. When this
happens, insights will be the reward. They almost always are.
Then, based on the response, Hugues will get to ask follow-up
questions and pull on the threads.
Hypothetically: maybe someone says, “I found an online class
which is like Peloton for judo, and I love it.” This would be perfect
avenue for Hugues to dig in deeper and perhaps ask a version of
this:
That’s interesting, thanks for sharing. Did you try any other online
classes before �nding this one? What do you love about this one
compared to others you may have tried? And I’m curious, what doesn’t
this online class o�er that you wish they did?
(Notice how the suggested response moved quickly from solution
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 14 --

back to investigating the problems more deeply — keep your eye
on the ball.)
These conversations can be wide-ranging and go in very different
directions based on the group and the person (or people) who
have contributed to the conversation.
Keep the questions problem/desire-focused. The Discover
Process’s primary purpose is to learn more about the
problems or desires plaguing a POP/MVA.
In the Validate Process, we’ll use these problems (jobs, pains, and
gains) to inform potential solutions which we’ll express as ‘Ma�a
Offers’ (i.e., offers to good to be refused). We’ll then go out and
validate our offer(s) by buying attention and measuring results.
But we’re not there yet.
For now, you want to �ll your mind with questions, not answers.
Open-ended questions are best (give your audience plenty of
room to tell you things you don’t already know).
Be careful with questions that contain embedded solutions. For
example, “have you ever considered improving your Judo skills
virtually?” is a question with an embedded solution.
A better, open-ended alternative, would be “how have Covid
lockdowns changed the way your practice Judo?”
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 14 --

Think about questions as canvases on which your audience can
paint their answers. The bigger the canvas, the more information
you’ll get.
“We live in worlds our questions create.” —Prof. David L.
Cooperrider, Case Western Reserve University.
Keep in mind that you want to capture the feedback you’re getting
and the insights you’re developing along the way. There’s a three-
part framework to do this work effectively.
Part I is collecting the raw information — screenshots, text, etc.
There are an in�nite number of tools for this from very simple
(Google Drive and Google Docs) to more complex (note taking
apps like Evernote, OneNote, Milanote, Roam, Notion, Obsidian,
etc).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 14 --

Milanote Empathy Map Template & Example
Start simple for minimum friction and maximum ef�ciency.
Part II is compilation. This is where you start to create separate
documents (or notes) that suggest an emergent theme. For
example, “virtual Judo instruction” might be a theme for Hugues.
Anytime someone mentions something related to virtual Judo
instruction, Hugues would copy that information to that
document or note.
Over time, the compilation notes may develop structure. For
example, placeholders for jobs, pains, and gains is a good place to
start. The goal is to capture the information in the language of the
audience, so copy comments (or other feedback) exactly as it was
written or spoken. The nuances of language matter.
Part III is where we transition from information to insights.
We’ve organized the raw information we’ve collected in Part I into
categories / themes in Part II. Now we start to ask ourselves “what
do I think this means?”
This is a thinking exercise, and there’s no right answer. The
information you collect will tell a story, and you’re in the role of
Sherlock Holmes trying to uncover what that story is about.
▪ What themes have the most feedback?
▪ Within those themes, what jobs, pains, and gains have the
most content?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 14 --

▪ Does everyone describe the jobs, pains, and gains the same
way, or are there nuances in the language?
Capture the insights from Part III in a single document or note.
This is what you’ll use when we move on to the Validate Process
(next).
Before You Move On Checklist (Discover Success
Criterion)
▪ 	I have identi�ed a speci�c interest, experience, and/or ability that
I can develop over time into a skill or expertise that I could enjoy
doing long-term.
▪ 	I have identi�ed a broad (not yet hyper-speci�c) POP/MVA who
will value the work I can create enough to (likely) pay for it.
▪ 	I have identi�ed 1-3 signi�cant problems (or desires) through
observational research that seem worth solving (from my audience’s
perspective).
▪ 	I have expressed these characteristics on a Value Proposition
Design Canvas (jobs, pains, and gains) and an Empathy Map (do,
see, say, hear).
▪ 	I have interacted and engaged with people who seem to make up
my ideal POP/MVA in places where they congregate, and I have
captured the information in the language they’ve expressed.
▪ 	I have updated my VPDC and EM with these new/deeper
insights.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 14 --

Ideally, you will have checked off every deliverable above before
moving on to the TDB Validate Process. However, only you know
what’s required for your speci�c situation, so use your best
judgment. If you’re 80% of the way through the list and feel like
you’re ready to move on successfully, then do that.
This is not a race. Remember: we’re going slow now, so we can go
much faster later. The checklist is a 10,000′ view of the ingredients
that lead to success — take that seriously and stack those
probabilities in your favor.
The Discover Process checklist is a �ywheel that builds quantity,
quality, and momentum simultaneously as you rotate around and
around until the list above has been checked off to a level that
feels right to you.
The �rst few passes may feel awkward and clunky as you �nd
your way. That’s normal and to be expected. Keep practicing, and
you’ll continue to build on small wins that lead to big successes.
▪ 	I feel I have enough insights to inform potential downstream
solutions based on veri�able objective facts, not just my subjective
ideas of reality.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 14 --

Pro Tip: Slight discomfort is the sweet-spot you’re aiming for. You
want to stretch but not break. If you’re too uncomfortable, overwhelm
will likely stall your momentum. No discomfort is a signal you have
more to give to the process; don’t sell yourself short.
Questions or feedback — please use the comments below. Use the
private accountability community to have discussions with your
peers and share your insights with them.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 14 --
